Search bar jump. STEPS: 
When searching inside a playlist (pull down from top) the initial search bar shows one line down from the header. 
BUT On tap it jumps one line above…. 😡
Often with a delay…leading to a second tap to initiate search. 
ISSUE: Results in accidentally selecting the first search result in the list 😞
Ruins your currently playing playlist selecting the wrong track on the new playlist. Ruining playing song for audience. 

Solutions:
- Search shows in the top line on pull down (same line as back)
- Or search stays on the second line (same place) the whole time. 
- Or start search result list one line down so one can’t select the first track by accident. 
- fix delay. Delay has been improved (worse on older devices) but still not good when searching your playlists to queue tracks for an audience

I’m the type of person who likes to arrange and rearrange the order of my playlists and to be able to categorise them into folders. By all of this you can only do on the desktop. Thank you for bringing attention to how the music industry has evolved — or perhaps, devolved — over the past decades.  However, I believe it's important to distinguish between the forces truly responsible for the degradation of musical authenticity and the role AI is now playing.  The pursuit of profit over artistic expression has been entrenched in the mainstream industry long before AI existed. For decades, music has been mass-produced, recycled, and commodified — often relying on samples, nostalgic formulas, and predictable structures, while sidelining genuine innovation and emotional depth.  AI didn’t take the soul out of music — it simply reflects the reality humanity had already shaped.  Blaming AI for the decline of cultural richness is a way of avoiding the harder truth: that as a collective, we allowed creativity to be replaced by marketability.  Perhaps this moment of crisis — amplified by AI and platforms like Spotify — will serve as a mirror, encouraging people to reconnect with authentic art, and with their own responsibility as listeners and creators.  Technology is a tool. It mirrors the intentions behind its use.  The question we should be asking is not "what AI is doing to us," but "what we have been doing to ourselves — and what we choose to do next."

I've been doing a deep-dive into your content since the YT algal ribbon recommended one of your videos to me - and bloody hell, it's excellent.  As a younger Gen X-er/Xennial and sound design /production nerd, I've always been an album person - I used to resent buying CD singles because the number of tracks didn't feel like great value for money at a time I was pretty skint (although I did buy them to support my favourite artists). I love the idea of an album as a coherent, cohestive, artistic whole that the artist has put creativity, effort, emotion and thought into creating. Even if they're not full-blown concept albums, a lot of albums have a kind of 'narrative' to them.  I admit that Spotify is convenient, and I'm one of those people you mentioned who pretty quickly switched to premium because I have such eclectic music tastes. I don't have a lot of disposable income, but I'm willing to put more of it these days towards Patreon subscriptions, Bandcamp, buying directly, or picking up vinyl from the merch desk at a live gig.  My brother runs a local music group that's a bit like a book club, but for albums - we meet physically in a pub once a month and listen to and discuss a different album each time. It also has a very active Whatsapp chat for between meetups. This has been a brilliant way for me to discover 'new to me' music in a way that's a lot more organic than algorithmic recommendations. Most of the group are younger Gen X/elder millennials, but there are a few younger/older members tricking in as well.

It started fine with songs from my liked songs such as Lonely Day by System of a Down and A Desolation Song by Agalloch both of which are somber songs, but part way through Spotify added I'm on a boat by the lonely island which is upbeat and silly. The algorithm picked up the word lonely in the bands name but was well designed enough to determine that the song didn't fit the mood. Plus Spotifys algorithm is supposedly able to analyze things like tempo, key, energy, and loudness but by all these metric I'm on a boat doesn't match any other song on the playlist. These algorithms don't understand words, they create mathematical equations that associate numerical values to words so they can be understood by a machine that at it's core is just doing algebra in binary but those machines are incapable of understanding context, double meanings, or non standard English among other things. This algorithm couldn't figure out that in  the context of what I was asking if for the name of a band is irrelevant. This is why it bothers me that LLM's are being called AI. They aren't intelligent, they are complex word calculators with no understanding of what they output.

Having used Spotify, Apple Music, and now YouTube Music, over the past 10 years, here's my take.  Spotify had the best music discovery feature for me, but could not justify spending the money on it, as the others were included in other bundles. Apple Music was the easiest to kind of just start and go without thinking too much, with good sound quality and ability to have downloaded music on the Apple Watch as well (useful when going for a run if you don't use watch cellular). I used it because it was included in Apple One which made financial sense for me at that time. YouTube Music has the best "create station" feature for me, quality is on par with Apple, the library is by far the biggest IMO, and already knows so much about me having used YouTube for so many years. Not as easy to start random playlists (interface makes it a bit more confusing as to what is in each of them or what is new), does not remember the previously played one if the app gets killed in the background, and no Apple Watch support (only as a remote, not player). But included in my YouTube Premium which is a no brainer anyway for someone spending time on YouTube, so definitely worth it overall.

Usé Apple Music durante 4 años y Spotify más de 10, y la diferencia de enfoque es evidente. Apple Music parece pensado para un consumo más generalista: las recomendaciones cambian poco, muchas veces reciclan lo ya conocido y rara vez sugieren discos realmente interesantes o inesperados. En macOS además se siente como una mezcla incompleta entre el viejo iTunes y una app moderna, con secciones que parecen simplemente páginas web dentro de la aplicación.  El audio Lossless existe, pero eso no significa automáticamente mejor experiencia. En varios álbumes la masterización o las versiones disponibles dan la sensación de estar optimizadas para impresionar  más que para una reproducción fiel en un sistema Hi-Fi. Con un equipo resolutivo esa diferencia se nota.  Spotify, con todos sus defectos, entiende mejor el descubrimiento musical: actualiza recomendaciones constantemente y el algoritmo aprende rápido. Esto, en la práctica, lo sabe todo el mundo y explica por qué sigue siendo la plataforma más masiva: están en otra liga en cuanto a playlists, curaduría y cuidado general de las recomendaciones.  Apple Music todavía arrastra problemas básicos de catalogación (artistas duplicados, álbumes repetidos, metadatos inconsistentes), algo difícil de aceptar viniendo de Apple. No es que sea una mala app; simplemente hoy parece más orientada al usuario mainstream que a quien escucha álbumes completos y busca descubrir música en serio.

Hey everyone, Thanks for reaching out about this to the Community. Note that there are several reasons why you might see the same song come up in your Discover Weekly more than once. One of them is that the song was released as part of multiple albums. Because of this it's identified by the system as a separate track. It's also possible that the song got removed and re-uploaded with a different version. In order avoid this from happenings and to get more diverse recommendations on your Discover Weekly in general, we suggest that you do the following: Make sure you're not listening to Spotify in Private Session Save your favorite songs and listen to music you like Unfollow your Discover Weekly playlist, and then follow it again under 'Browse > Discover' By following these steps and expanding your music collection you can give the algorithm a little push and should start seeing new suggestions, more in synch with your taste after a couple of integrations of your Discover Weekly. In order to be able to better address your individual issues with the playlist, we're closing off this thread for new comments. If you continue to experience any difficulties, you can always create a new thread in the relevant help board. Cheers!

Dear Spotify, I am writing to address a concern that has significant cultural and emotional implications for many of your users, particularly in Ukraine. As you are aware, the ongoing imperialistic russian war in Ukraine, has had profound and painful effects on the lives of millions of Ukrainians. The war, marked by severe trauma, loss, and displacement, has deeply impacted the Ukrainian people's relationship with Russian language and culture. In this context, a lot of Ukrainian users of Spotify have reported the platform's algorithm frequently recommending Russian content. This includes users who have not previously engaged with such content or have actively avoided it. For many, the Russian language has become inextricably linked to the suffering and anguish caused by the russians. Consequently, encountering Russian content on a platform intended for entertainment and relaxation can be distressing and triggering. While I recognize Spotify's commitment to offering a diverse range of content to its global audience, I believe it is crucial to consider the unique and sensitive situation faced by Ukrainian users. To address this, I propose the following solutions: Opt-Out Feature for Specific Content : Implement a feature allowing users to opt out of content from specific languages or regions. This will enable Ukrainian users to avoid Russian content and create a more comfortable and personalized listening experience. Sensitive Content Recommendations : Enhance the recommendation algorithm to be more sensitive to the geopolitical context. This could involve reducing the frequency of Russian content suggested to users in Ukraine or those who have a history of engaging with Ukrainian content. User Feedback Integration : Incorporate user feedback mechanisms to understand and adapt to individual preferences, especially in regions experiencing conflict or tension. I understand that music and podcasts are universal languages that transcend borders and conflicts. However, in times of turmoil and strife, the cultural and linguistic nuances become more pronounced and carry a deeper significance. My request is not about limiting cultural exchange but about providing a safe and considerate listening environment for all your users, respecting their emotional and cultural contexts. I hope Spotify will consider this request seriously and take steps to ensure its platform remains a source of comfort and enjoyment for everyone, regardless of their geopolitical situation. Thank you for your attention to this important matter. UPD To everyone who’s commented along the lines of, “You can’t expect Spotify to get rid of an entire language of music just because it offends you. With that logic, they should remove every language that has caused harm to anyone”: please read the post carefully. We are simply asking for an option that allows users to configure languages they prefer not to hear. This does not mean we are asking Spotify to remove that content from the platform altogether.

Hi, I tweeted previously about the new homepage UI on mobile and got some canned responses. I tried to DM directly to @spotifycares saying I would really like an actual dialogue with some engagement from their side with some actual answers: I can see that there are many unhappy folks in the support board and on twitter. I am a web developer, and as such I don’t normally make comments about App or software changes because usually I either agree or can at least see good reason for the changes. In this case I cannot see a good reason for any of the changes and feel I have to provide actual feedback: New recommendation tile takes whole screen and there is no defined reason for why its being recommended, eg category, or theme or ‘because you like/listened to X’ Why is there a like button on it? Why would I like something ‘new’ without listening to it first Whole UI is worse for new / similar music discovery autoplaying video is bad for focus and data usage No way to disable in the settings (eg Settings > Video Quality.) Does not respect the existing setting in Settings > Payback > Canvas Why would you put a stream of autoplaying videos in the homepage of an app that’s main purpose is for music listening - obvious answer is promotion of other services (audiobooks, podcasts etc). If you scroll down anywhere past the first one, and actually tap on the tile to view the album /artist / playlist and then come back to the homepage it starts at the top again and does not remember the place you were originally at. This makes it even more annoying. Why not add a new ‘feed’ tab but keep the homepage for everyone else who actually find it useful. I think it’s good to test software, and betas. And this has great benefits if done right. In this case though I think this has been handled very badly: You made no mention of the change when it happened. You gave me no easy way to provide feedback without me having to search out ways to provide it. You give no way to opt out of this test or future tests. All these canned responses to all the twitter threads and support channels make me feel like no-one is really listening I am in the Apple ecosystem, and using Apple Music would probably be easier for me than using Spotify (only exception would be the Amazon devices), but I find that Spotify has better discovery for new / similar music. With the new home page feed this is no longer the case. I received a response: Thanks for keeping in touch. Currently, we don't have any further info to provide, but we're here if you need help with anything else.

I constantly find my Release Radar recommending songs by an artist (say A) with the same name as an artist (say A') that I might actually wanna listen to. This is extremely dumb as an issue because these two artists are listed as genuinely different artists in Spotify and the newly recommended song by a wrong artist is listed as a song of A' in the system. A reasonable conclusion is that at least Release Radar does not look into the artist IDs but just merely refers to their names. This happens to like 5 different artists to me and my Release Radar is contaminated by songs which I have absolutely no interest in. I believe this is a very basic bug that can be fixed in like 5 minutes.

Here's an as short as possible version of a previous post in this thread, explaining the behavior of the Release Radar. I receive tracks from artists I don't follow at all in my Release Radar - This is intended and we don't plan on changing this. The Release Radar doesn't only help you keep track of artists you follow, but is meant to allow you to discover new music from others we think you might enjoy. It is also one way for Spotify to help artists get their music noticed. I get music that I don't enjoy at all in my Release Radar - This means our algorithm doesn't know your music taste so well. There can be many reasons behind this, but the rule of thumb to improve the music you receive in the automatically generated playlists for you is to listen to music without a private session, use the like and hide features, have more intentional music listening habits rather than opportunity based and when you discover something new that you enjoy, listen to it proportionally more than other content. Following these guidelines should give you better recommendations in time. It will still not be perfect and it can vary from person to person, but we're constantly improving these as we go. I get tracks from artists that are named exactly the same as ones I follow and enjoy, but it turns out the track is unrelated to that artist and they only have the same name. - This happens if a track initially gets erroneously uploaded to the artist you follow. It can be that the error is already corrected by the time your Release Radar updates, but it already captured the track on its upload. It can happen with featured artists as well. Currently, there's no technical solution in place than can prevent errors like this happening, as we rely on submitted metadata. If you spot an incorrect content, it's best to report it as described here . Make sure your report is clear. If you can't find an artist profile with the same name as the one you follow, it probably hasn't been created yet and is part of the reason the error occurred. Point that out in your report to ensure it can be processed efficiently.

Enterprising souls with a little patience can automate playlists through IFTTT, I used it for years to automatically capture an archive of Release Radar and Discover Weekly. Still, the sorting has worked.

Spotify increasingly feels stuck in a loop. The algorithm relies heavily on what we’ve listened to in the past and keeps pushing the same artists, the same songs, the same vibes over and over again. Instead of opening doors to new discoveries, it locks us into a comfortable but repetitive bubble. By prioritizing what already worked, Spotify ends up freezing our musical tastes in the past and slowing down real exploration, when music should be about curiosity, renewal, and surprise.

I use Spotify playlists throughout my workday to keep the office humming and productivity up. But, since the launch of "Made for You" playlists, all of my playlists have pretty much warped into monoliths of one another. I used to have multiple Spotify playlists within a genre that were unique and would break up the monotony of the workday. But now, multiple playlists are playing the same songs, so they all sound the same and it's hard to find something to listen to that we haven't heard dozens of times. It really defeats the purpose of having for a Spotify account, which I expect to deliver me unique music with niche distinction. Please give us the option to turn OFF the "Made for You" playlists so that we can enjoy curated song lists that deliver new and varied music, instead of derivate and predictable algorithmically-generated playlists.
